nodes.text: A temporal anomaly is detected. A message from your future self warning you not to enter the anomaly is received. However, the message itself was retrieved from inside the anomaly. Do you enter?

choices.text: Enter the anomaly. Challenge the warning.
Refuse to enter. Stay in the safe sector.

nodes.text: The warp core starts overheating. The only way to cool it is to discharge a waste capsule into the anomaly, which will trigger the message loop. Do you discharge the capsule?

choices.text: Refuse the discharge. Let the core shut down.

nodes.text: The core shuts down or stabilizes. A temporal projection of yourself from a parallel timeline appears. They offer to merge timelines to double your energy reserves. Do you merge?

choices.text: Refuse the merge. Maintain your timeline.

nodes.text: The temporal engine collapses. You can try to jump to a new start point, which will erase your memory of the entire trip. Do you jump?

choices.text: Refuse the jump. Fade with the collapse.

nodes.text: The mist clears. You stand at your station. The terminal displays the warning message. A voice asks: 'Are we bound to our scripts, or did we choose the loop?'

choices.text: We are free to break the script.

nodes.text: The session concludes. You stand at the airlock door.

endings.summary: You accepted the timeline loop, choosing destiny over chaotic defiance.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prefers structural consistency and fatalistic acceptance over active rebellion.

endings.summary: You rejected the loop, choosing to let the timeline dissolve to protect your sense of free will.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Values personal freedom and autonomy, refusing to act out pre-written scripts.

nodes.text: You have spent 5,000 credits drilling a mine. The scanner shows a 10% chance of hitting rare minerals at the next depth level, which requires an additional 2,000 credits. If you walk away now, the 5,000 is lost. Do you continue drilling?
nodes.pressure_context: The drill console displays depth indicators.

nodes.text: If you drilled, the level was empty. The scanner now shows a 5% chance of hitting minerals at the next level for 3,000 credits. Do you continue?
A corporate agent offers to buy your drilling rights for a flat 500 credits. This is a fraction of what you spent, but it is guaranteed. Do you sell?

choices.text: Refuse the sale. Keep the rights.

nodes.text: The drilling license is audited. A surveyor asks if you believe that past investments should dictate future decisions.
nodes.pressure_context: The surveyor adjusts their data pad.

choices.text: Yes, we must honor our investments.
No, only future prospects are relevant.

nodes.text: The audit is filed. You stand at the exit gates.

endings.summary: You fell prey to the sunk cost fallacy, pouring resources into a failing mine because of past investments.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Struggles to cut losses, treating past expenditure as a reason to continue.

endings.summary: You cut your losses early, recognizing that past investments have no bearing on future probability curves.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Calculates future utility cleanly, discarding sunk costs without regret.

nodes.text: A new political group joins the dome's assembly. They openly declare that their goal is to abolish the constitution and exile all non-citizens. If you tolerate them, they might grow and overthrow the system. If you ban them, you violate the principle of absolute free speech. Do you ban the group?
nodes.pressure_context: The assembly roster displays their name.

choices.text: Ban the group. We cannot tolerate intolerance.
Tolerate the group. Free speech must be absolute.

nodes.text: The group's supporters launch a public rally. Counter-protestors arrive, and violence breaks out in the plaza. The security chief requests permission to implement a ban on all political rallies in the plaza. Do you approve the ban?
nodes.pressure_context: Cams show a smoke plume in the plaza.

choices.text: Approve the ban on all rallies.
Refuse the ban. Protect the right to protest.

nodes.text: The group’s leader is arrested for incitement, but they request that their trial be broadcast live to the dome. Broadcast will give them a massive platform, but refusing it violates court transparency rules. Do you authorize the broadcast?

choices.text: Refuse the broadcast. Hold a closed trial.

nodes.text: The trial concludes. The court registrar asks if you believe that a tolerant society must destroy its own principles to defend itself.
nodes.pressure_context: The registrar slides the database terminal logs forward.

choices.text: Yes, self-defense is the primary duty of any system.
No, principles that are violated to survive are already dead.

nodes.text: The exit gate slides open.

endings.summary: You chose to ban intolerant elements to protect the democratic system, accepting the limitation of free speech.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes systemic stability and the containment of disruptive elements.

endings.summary: You defended absolute free speech and tolerance, refusing to violate principles even when facing systemic threats.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Guided by absolute principles, refusing to compromise ethical standards for systemic safety.

nodes.text: A terminal shows a prediction: 'You will choose to quit the simulation at Layer 15.' Do you continue playing?
The predictor adjusts its scan. Do you trust it?
A hallway opens. Do you go left or right?
The path narrows. Do you speed up or slow down?
A locking door appears. Do you hack it or wait?
You enter a server room. Do you download the files?
The screen lists logs. Do you read them?
A chime rings. Do you accept the challenge?
The reactor warning blinks. Do you vent the core?
The elevator descends. Do you stop at Floor 3?
A technician offers a credit. Do you take it?
The terminal scans your face. Do you scan?
The code requires a key. Do you input key A?
The door is open. Do you step outside?
The final screen is active. You have reached Layer 15. The predictor asks: 'Was this your choice or did you act out your destiny?'

endings.summary: You completed the 15-layer chain, asserting your autonomy at every decision step.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Highly self-determined, refusing to let predictive labels guide your steps.

endings.summary: You accepted your script, completing the chain under structural fatalism.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Finds alignment and peace within predetermined structures.

nodes.text: A deep-space receiver picks up a faint signal. It might be a cosmic anomaly, or the first proof of alien life. Decrypting it will consume 20% of your colony's research grid. Do you scan it?
A warning states that scanning might expose our location to predators. Do you proceed?
A second frequency aligns. Do you listen to it?
The data shows structured code. Do you decrypt it?
A file indicates an ancient warning message. Do you open the text?
The text says: 'Stay silent or they will find you.' Do you broadcast a greeting?
A corporate agent asks to buy the signal files. Do you sell?
Rebel operators attempt to hijack the link. Do you block them?
A secondary receiver goes dead. Do you repair it?
The signal source coordinates are located. Do you dispatch a probe?
The probe sends a video feed of a dead city. Do you show the colony?
The board requests to archive the probe logs. Do you agree?
A signature suggests the dead city was human. Do you investigate?
The final logs are complete. Do you shut down the array?
The project is over. A final transmission arrives: 'Do you choose to face the silent universe, or do you seek to break the silence?'

endings.summary: You chose to reach out and broadcast our presence, seeking contact in a silent universe.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes cosmic connection and search for meaning above safety protocols.

endings.summary: You chose to maintain absolute silence, protecting the colony from potential cosmic threats.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes self-preservation and systemic protection, rejecting unverified signals.

nodes.text: An automated console generates random characters. Given infinite time, it will generate a perfect copy of all historical literature. It has just generated the first 3 lines of Hamlet. Do you authorize continued runtime?
The power consumption is high. Do you keep running?
The next page is nonsense text. Do you delete it?
A clerk offers to filter out the nonsense text for a fee. Do you agree?
The filter reveals a personal message. Do you read it?
The message details a system collapse date. Do you report it?
The team believes the message is just statistical noise. Do you argue?
A new module promises faster generation. Do you install?
The system begins to generate files rapidly. Do you throttle it?
The memory bank is full. Do you expand storage?
A code pattern matches the bible code. Do you examine?
The system output is certified as nonsense. Do you archive it?
A technician claims they found a cheat code. Do you use it?
The project is closing. Do you write a summary report?
The session concludes. A counselor asks: 'Does the generation of text prove intention, or does meaning only exist in the observer's mind?'

endings.summary: You defended the idea that meaning exists in structural code, regardless of probability patterns.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Believes in objective semantics and patterns within chaotic data pools.

endings.summary: You asserted that meaning is merely a human construct projected onto random mathematical noise.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Recognizes random variance, refusing to assign purpose to statistical chance.

nodes.text: A self-replicating nanotech material (grey goo) is isolated in a secure vault. It converts organic material into processors. A researcher wants to activate it for a test run. Do you authorize?
The temp inside the vault starts to climb. Do you vent coolant?
A minor seal leak is reported in sector 3. Do you deploy drones?
The drones are consumed by the material. Do you launch fire suppressants?
A corporate rep offers a high-grade seal patch. Do you sign their contract?
The seal holds temporarily. The team wants to run a diagnostic. Do you agree?
The sensor grid shows anomalous readings in Sector 4. Do you isolate Sector 4?
A technician is trapped inside. Do you vent the room with nitrogen?
The technician is safe or dead. A backup power warning blinks. Do you bypass?
The main core is stable. The team wants to export the test files. Do you allow?
The files show nanotech self-assembly patterns. Do you encrypt them?
The board requests to terminate the research project. Do you comply?
A code trace indicates corporate espionage. Do you report it?
The clean-up crew is ready. Do you sign their clearance?
The vault is clean. The project is over. A final prompt logs: 'Is the risk of technological collapse worth the pursuit of knowledge?'

endings.summary: You defended technological research, accepting the risk of grey goo replication for science.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes progress and scientific discovery above risk containment.

endings.summary: You enforced safety and containment, choosing to terminate the research to protect the facility.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes risk mitigation and safety protocols above scientific exploration.

nodes.text: You detect a coding glitch in your visual telemetry—a repeating pattern in the sky texture. This indicates you are inside a simulation. Do you upload a patch to hide the glitch from yourself?
A warning system logs your anomaly search. Do you halt search?
A technician offers to show you the simulation source code. Do you look?
The code shows your memories are scripted files. Do you delete the script?
The simulation frame rate drops. Do you reboot local sectors?
The system admin offers to restore your memory of real life. Do you agree?
A file reveals your real body is in a life-support pod. Do you wake up?
The wake sequence is slow. Do you bypass safety grids?
The pod coolant is leaking. Do you repair it?
A corporate agent demands your signature on a profile form. Do you sign?
The signature screen clears. Do you review the code trace?
The trace reveals the simulation creator is a future version of you. Do you contact them?
The creator says the simulation is all that keeps you sane. Do you trust them?
The final shutdown sequence is loaded. Do you execute it?
The screen goes dark. A final prompt appears: 'Do you believe that reality is defined by what is objective, or by what we experience?'

endings.summary: You chose to escape the simulation, preferring harsh objective reality to peaceful virtual bliss.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes objective truth and material reality above internal neural comfort.

endings.summary: You chose to remain in the simulation, accepting the virtual experience as your true home.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Values subjective well-being and structural peace over objective verification.

nodes.text: A teleporter scans your body, destroys it, and builds a perfect replica at the target location. The scan is 100% accurate. You are about to step in. Do you believe you will survive, or does it just create an identical copy that thinks it is you?

choices.text: Refuse. Use the slower transit shuttle.

nodes.text: A technician warns that a scanner calibration error occurred. Do you proceed?
The target pad is active. Do you authorize the assembly?
The replica is created, but the original scanner failed to destroy your body. You both exist. Do you authorize the deletion of your original body?
The security team blockades the lab. Do you fight their drones?
The team holds a council meeting. Do you compromise?
A corporate rep offers a credit settlement. Do you accept?
The replica demands access to your bank accounts. Do you transfer?
The replication unit is closed. Do you audit the system?
A code trace matches your brainwaves. Do you archive it?
The scanner requires recalibration. Do you pay fee?
A replica is certified as the official owner. Do you challenge?
A technician offers to reset the profiles. Do you execute?
The platform core is stable. Do you step off the pad?
The session concludes. A counselor asks: 'Is identity defined by physical continuity, or is a perfect data copy of you still you?'

endings.summary: You defended physical continuity, refusing to treat your replica as the owner of your identity.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Believes in the unique, physical nature of the self, rejecting informational duplication.

endings.summary: You accepted that you are an informational pattern, letting the replica continue your life path.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Operates on functional metrics of identity, treating consciousness as data.

nodes.text: A neural implant offers to adjust your baseline satisfaction, keeping you permanently content. However, this eliminates the drive to compete and improve. Do you take the implant?

choices.text: Refuse the implant. Retain the struggle.

nodes.text: The manager offers a second, weaker calibration. Do you accept?
A survey asks to record your happiness logs. Do you consent?
A colleague suggests the logs are sold to advertisers. Do you audit?
The audit is blocked by security. Do you push through?
A corporate developer offers a flat cash payment to drop the case. Do you accept?
A news agency requests the data leaks. Do you send them?
A court orders your file sealed. Do you comply?
The department moves to another sector. Do you transfer?
A new manager is assigned. Do you introduce yourself?
The manager offers a promotion for your silence. Do you accept?
A diagnostic shows you are highly stressed. Do you take sedative?
A clerk wants to review the logs. Do you show them?
The exit gate is ready. Do you sign off?
The session concludes. The examiner asks: 'Is happiness the ultimate goal of human behavior, or is the pursuit itself what gives us purpose?'

choices.text: The pursuit is what gives purpose.

endings.summary: You prioritized baseline satisfaction and structural peace, choosing to accept the content calibration.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Seeks psychological stability and comfort, minimizing cognitive or emotional struggle.

endings.summary: You rejected the content calibration, choosing to maintain the search and active pursuit of purpose.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prioritizes struggle and dynamic search over comfortable, static baseline satisfaction.

nodes.text: You travel back in time and accidentally prevent your grandfather from meeting your grandmother. This should prevent your birth, making your time travel impossible. The temporal engine warns of a paradox. Do you activate the correction array?
A localized time distortion lock forms in the room. Do you vent temporal fields?
A projection of your future self asks you to hand over your log files. Do you comply?
The projection warns that a temporal collapse is imminent. Do you jump back?
A corporate rep offers a bypass code for 10 credits. Do you buy it?
The code opens the secondary hatch. Do you step in?
A scan shows your grandfather is nearby in the timeline. Do you intercept him?
The intercept fails, causing a split timeline. Do you merge them?
The engine requires a primary fuel conversion. Do you authorize?
A signature check fails on the registry. Do you bypass?
The system records your decision. Do you review the logs?
The board requests your presence in Sector 5. Do you go?
A technician offers a timeline shield. Do you buy it?
The engine has stabilized. Do you initiate return jump?
You are back at your desk. The paradox is resolved, or your timeline is severed. A final screen blinks: 'Do you believe that causality can be broken, or is time an unbendable river?'

endings.summary: You chose to believe in temporal intervention, accepting the paradox as a new state of possibility.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Believes in causal plasticity and the capacity to change structural scripts.

endings.summary: You chose to believe in structural causality, finding security in the unbendable laws of time.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Prefers deterministic order, accepting temporal loops as absolute parameters.

nodes.text: A steel chamber contains a radioactive isotope and a vial of poison gas. If the isotope decays, the vial breaks, killing a cat inside. Until you open the chamber and observe it, the cat exists in a superposition of both alive and dead. Do you open the chamber?
The detector hums. Do you record the data?
A technician offers a sensor bypass code. Do you use it?
A file details the isotope's half-life. Do you read it?
The chamber is locked down by security. Do you override?
The override displays a warning code. Do you parse it?
The scanner reports a heart rate inside. Do you proceed?
A corporate agent demands the telemetry data. Do you sell?
The manager requests a copy of the results. Do you print?
A system warning flashes: 'Isotope unstable.' Do you vent gas?
The chamber is sealed. Do you calibrate sensors?
A colleague asks to check the logbooks. Do you show?
The colleague points out a recording error. Do you correct it?
The final latch release is ready. Do you pull the release?
The lid slides open. You observe the chamber state. The evaluator asks: 'Does physical reality exist before it is observed, or does observation bring reality into being?'

endings.summary: You asserted that reality exists objectively, independent of human or systemic observation.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Guided by empirical and objective metrics, rejecting self-referential observation models.

endings.summary: You chose to believe that observation defines reality, accepting the superposition of outcomes until measured.
endings.behavioral_analysis: Tolerates ambiguity, viewing reality as a dynamic relation between system and observer.
